review.summary: Patch 1 looks correct, but patch 2 breaks IPSR.PMIP write semantics. The new PMIP branch clears on write-0 and does nothing on write-1, which is the inverse of the intended RW1C behavior and inconsistent with the existing IPSR handler pattern.

A.summary: Reviewed the cover letter and both diffs; scope is limited to HPM timer setup and IPSR PMIP handling.

B.summary: Patch 1 was checked for local logic consistency. Resetting irq_overflow_left before recomputing the new timer state matches the described stale-state bug and does not introduce an obvious regression.

C.summary: Patch 2 was checked against the existing IPSR update pattern for CIP/FIP/PIP. PMIP handling does not follow the same write path and appears inverted.

D.summary: Cross-checked expected PMIP clearing behavior against existing public usage: software clears PMIP by writing 1 to IPSR.PMIP, reinforcing that the new condition is backward.

E.summary: Series should not be applied as-is. Fix the PMIP condition and revalidate IPSR write behavior for all pending bits.

findings.title: IPSR.PMIP RW1C handling is inverted
findings.description: The new PMIP path uses `if (!(data & RISCV_IOMMU_IPSR_PMIP))`, which means a write of 0 clears PMIP and a write of 1 leaves it unchanged. That is the opposite of RW1C semantics described in the commit message. It is also inconsistent with the existing `riscv_iommu_update_ipsr()` pattern for CIP/FIP/PIP, where the written bit being 1 triggers recomputation/clearing. As written, guest software that writes 1 to clear PMIP will fail to clear it.
findings.suggestion: Mirror the existing IPSR bit handling and trigger PMIP processing when `data & RISCV_IOMMU_IPSR_PMIP` is set, not when it is clear. If PMIP needs conditional reassertion based on HPM state, implement that inside the positive branch the same way CIP/FIP/PIP are handled.
findings.confidence_reason: The surrounding IPSR handler uses positive bit tests for the other pending bits, and public Linux RISC-V IOMMU code clears PMIP by writing `RISCV_IOMMU_IPSR_PMIP` to IPSR. Both indicate the added negated condition is wrong.

disclaimer: LLM-generated draft. Not an authoritative review.
